sections.heading: MMR 2 improved, but many children still missed the full schedule
sections.body: Among children aged 24 to 35 months, MMR 2 coverage rose from 66.4 percent in 2022 to 74.2 percent in 2025. Full vaccination according to the national schedule also increased, from 49.9 to 55.0 percent. In 2025, the gap between the two measures was 19.2 percentage points, which shows that getting MMR 2 is not the same as completing the full schedule. Many children received the second measles-containing dose but still did not finish the broader national schedule.

sections.heading: Top regions differed for full vaccination and MMR 2
sections.body: The National Capital Region had the highest full-schedule completion in 2025 at 68.2 percent. It was followed by Davao Region at 66.7 percent, Central Luzon at 64.5 percent, and Ilocos Region at 64.4 percent. But Ilocos Region had the highest MMR 2 coverage by a wide margin, at 95.6 percent. This shows that a region can do very well on MMR 2 while still having many children who did not complete the full national schedule.

sections.heading: Some regions had large gaps after MMR 2
sections.body: SOCCSKSARGEN had 69.0 percent MMR 2 coverage in 2025, but only 37.3 percent full-schedule completion, a gap of 31.7 percentage points. Ilocos Region had a similarly wide gap, with 95.6 percent for MMR 2 and 64.4 percent for full-schedule completion. Western Visayas and the Negros Island Region also had high MMR 2 coverage, at 84.7 and 86.3 percent, but lower full-schedule completion, at 58.0 and 60.3 percent. These gaps show that finishing the full schedule is a separate challenge from delivering MMR 2.

sections.heading: BARMM remained lowest on both measures
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest coverage on both measures in 2025, with 34.8 percent MMR 2 coverage and 14.3 percent full-schedule completion. MMR 2 improved from 26.0 percent in 2022, but full-schedule completion stayed at 14.3 percent. This suggests that progress on MMR 2 did not carry over into broader schedule completion in the region.

sections.heading: Some regions lost ground after 2022
sections.body: Cagayan Valley fell from 96.3 percent MMR 2 coverage in 2022 to 64.6 percent in 2025. Its full-schedule completion also dropped, from 83.5 to 44.4 percent. The Cordillera Administrative Region declined on both measures as well, from 76.7 to 59.1 percent for MMR 2 and from 59.3 to 40.3 percent for full-schedule completion. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ declined from 56.3 to 50.3 percent for MMR 2 and from 47.7 to 35.9 percent for full-schedule completion. These declines show that the national increase hides sizable regional setbacks.

sections.heading: Differences between girls and boys were small
sections.body: In 2025, female children had slightly higher MMR 2 coverage than male children, at 74.9 percent compared with 73.5 percent. Full-schedule completion was slightly higher among male children, at 55.7 percent compared with 54.2 percent among female children. The gaps are small, and the higher group changes depending on the measure, so sex was not a major divider in this vaccination profile.

sections.heading: Later-born children were less likely to finish the full schedule
sections.body: First-born children had the highest full-schedule completion in 2025 at 63.4 percent. Children of birth order 6 or higher had the lowest, at 41.8 percent. MMR 2 coverage changed less sharply, from 77.2 percent among first-born children to 70.0 percent among children of birth order 6 or higher. The wider gap for full-schedule completion suggests that later-born children were especially less likely to finish the broader schedule, even when many still received MMR 2.

sections.heading: Children with seen vaccination cards had much higher coverage
sections.body: Children whose vaccination card was seen had 88.5 percent MMR 2 coverage and 74.0 percent full-schedule completion in 2025. Among children who never had a card, the levels were much lower, at 31.0 and 14.4 percent. Children whose card was not seen or who no longer had a card were in between, at 61.0 percent for MMR 2 and 34.7 percent for full-schedule completion. This does not prove that the card itself caused vaccination, but it shows that documented contact with the vaccination system was strongly linked with completion.

sections.heading: Urban children led on MMR 2, but not much on full completion
sections.body: Urban children had 76.6 percent MMR 2 coverage in 2025, compared with 71.4 percent among rural children. Full-schedule completion was much closer, at 55.8 percent in urban areas and 54.2 percent in rural areas. This pattern suggests that the urban advantage was clearer for MMR 2 than for the full national schedule.

sections.heading: Mother's education showed mixed but real gaps
sections.body: Children whose mothers had Grades 7 to 10 education had the highest usable full-schedule completion in 2025 at 59.9 percent. Those whose mothers had Grades 1 to 6 education had 46.7 percent. MMR 2 coverage was highest among children whose mothers had college education, at 77.6 percent, but their full-schedule completion was 53.9 percent. The no-education row was flagged in the display data, with 49.8 percent for MMR 2 and 33.5 percent for full-schedule completion. The pattern is not a simple step-by-step rise with education, but lower maternal schooling and flagged no-education values show weaker completion.

sections.heading: Children in the lowest wealth group still lagged
sections.body: Children in the lowest wealth quintile had 66.6 percent MMR 2 coverage and 49.3 percent full-schedule completion in 2025. The highest quintile had the highest MMR 2 coverage at 81.9 percent, but its full-schedule completion was 54.8 percent. The middle quintile had the highest full-schedule completion at 61.0 percent. The pattern does not rise neatly across every quintile, but the lowest quintile was still clearly behind on both measures.

takeaways: National MMR 2 coverage rose to 74.2 percent in 2025, but full vaccination according to the national schedule reached only 55.0 percent.
The 19.2-point national gap between MMR 2 and full-schedule completion shows that getting MMR 2 does not mean a child completed the broader schedule.
Regional patterns were uneven: BARMM was lowest on both measures, and several regions had wide gaps between MMR 2 and full-schedule completion.
Vaccination card status showed the strongest divide: completion was much higher among children whose card was seen than among children who never had a card.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
